planting.note: Plant in spring to enjoy the vivid gold spring flush.

directions: In spring this classic hosta emerges with bright yellow leaf centers edged in green that gradually fade to soft green by summer. Plant the crown level with the soil in rich, moist ground. Lavender flowers open in midsummer above the settled green mound.

planting.note: Plant in spring; reliable and easy in most shade gardens.

directions: This dependable green hosta carries a thin, crisp white edge on each leaf and forms a neat spreading mound. Set the crown at grade in moist, organic soil and mulch to hold moisture. Violet flowers appear on upright scapes in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade to preserve the blue waxy coating.

directions: This massive hosta lifts huge, corrugated blue-green leaves on strong stems like a canopy of umbrellas. Plant the crown level with the soil in deep, fertile ground and give it plenty of room. Keep it in shade to protect the powdery blue bloom, and enjoy near-white flowers in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in bright shade to hold the white centers.

directions: Named for its cherry-red flower scapes, this small hosta pairs creamy-white leaf centers with green margins. Plant the crown at grade in moist, humus-rich soil. Lavender flowers rise on the striking red stems in summer, and seed pods take on a reddish cast to extend the show.

planting.note: Plant in spring in bright shade in fertile soil.

directions: This bold, thick-leaved hosta shows wide blue-green margins wrapping a broad creamy-gold center. Plant the crown level with the soil in rich, moisture-holding ground. The heavy substance resists slugs, and near-white flowers appear on tall scapes in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; tolerates warmth and humidity well.

directions: A member of the fragrant plantain-lily group, this large green hosta forms a glossy mound and thrives in warmer gardens. Plant the crown at grade in rich, evenly moist soil. Sweetly scented near-white flowers open on tall scapes in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; light exposure intensifies the gold color.

directions: This small, quick-spreading gold hosta forms a bright cushion of heart-shaped leaves ideal for edging or rock gardens. Plant the crown level with the soil in moist, well-drained ground. Lavender flowers rise on slender scapes in midsummer above the golden mound.

planting.note: Plant in spring in bright shade to hold the gold coloring.

directions: This compact gold hosta holds slightly cupped, puckered leaves that brighten with morning light. Plant the crown at grade in rich, moisture-retentive soil and keep watered during heat. Near-white flowers open on upright scapes in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; spreads by runners to knit a groundcover.

directions: This spreading hosta runs on stolons to form a dense mat, making it useful as a shady groundcover or edging. Plant divisions at grade in moist soil and let them fill in. Green leaves with wavy cream margins carry deep purple flowers on tall scapes in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade to preserve the intense blue.

directions: One of the bluest of the Tardiana hostas, this compact mound holds thick, heart-shaped leaves with a heavy waxy bloom. Plant the crown level with the soil in cool, moist ground and keep it out of hot sun to protect the blue. Pale lavender flowers appear in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; tolerates more sun than many hostas.

directions: Grown for its fragrance, this large green hosta forms a big mound of wavy, glossy leaves. Plant the crown at grade in rich, evenly moist soil. Sweetly scented pale lavender flowers open on tall scapes in late summer and draw pollinators to the shade garden.

planting.note: Plant in spring; unusually tolerant of heat and drought.

directions: This tough hosta forms a low mound of thick, glossy green leaves with wavy edges and shrugs off heat better than most. Plant the crown level with the soil in well-drained ground. Fragrant pale lavender flowers appear on arching scapes in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade to keep the center variegation clean.

directions: This small hosta shows a striking creamy-white center that feathers into a broad green margin like a paisley pattern. Plant the crown at grade in moist, humus-rich soil. Lavender flowers rise on short scapes in midsummer above the patterned mound.

planting.note: Plant in spring; multiplies quickly into a tidy clump.

directions: This dwarf hosta forms a dense cushion of narrow chartreuse leaves that glow in shade. Plant the crown level with the soil in moist, well-drained ground and divide easily to increase. It reblooms freely, sending up lavender flowers repeatedly through summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in bright shade for the deepest gold.

directions: This little gold hosta holds cupped, metallic-sheened leaves on a compact mound perfect for the front of a shady border. Plant the crown at grade in rich, moist soil. Lavender flowers open on short scapes in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade to protect the blue coloring.

directions: This handsome blue hosta forms a mound of thick, deeply cupped and puckered leaves with excellent substance. Plant the crown level with the soil in cool, moist ground and shelter from hot sun to keep the blue bloom. Near-white flowers rise above the foliage in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in bright shade to bring out the warm center tones.

directions: This colorful hosta opens with golden leaf centers that flush warm apricot-orange in cool spring weather, framed by blue-green margins. Plant the crown at grade in rich, moist soil. Lavender flowers appear in midsummer above the glowing mound.

planting.note: Plant in spring in dappled shade in fertile soil.

directions: This lively hosta forms an upright, twisting mound of narrow green leaves edged in creamy yellow. Plant the crown level with the soil in moist, humus-rich ground. Lavender flowers rise on tall scapes in midsummer above the arching, vase-shaped clump.

planting.note: Plant in spring; vigorous and adaptable in most shade.

directions: A fast-growing, adaptable hosta with green leaves and bright creamy margins that light up shaded corners. Plant the crown at grade in moist, well-drained soil. Lavender flowers appear on upright scapes in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade to keep the blue and white markings.

directions: This distinctive hosta washes its thick blue-green leaves with splashes and streaks of milky white. Plant the crown level with the soil in cool, moist ground and keep it shaded to hold the coloring. Near-white flowers rise above the foliage in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; tolerates warmth and gives late scent.

directions: This large hosta forms an upright mound of green leaves rimmed in creamy white and blooms for fragrance. Plant the crown at grade in rich, evenly moist soil. Sweetly scented lavender flowers open on tall scapes in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade to preserve the blue margin and gold center.

directions: A sport of June, this hosta reverses the pattern with a bright gold center held inside wide blue-green margins. Plant the crown level with the soil in cool, moist ground. The thick leaves resist slugs, and pale lavender flowers appear in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade to protect the white leaf centers.

directions: This bright hosta carries broad white leaf centers framed by rippled green margins for strong contrast. Plant the crown at grade in moist, humus-rich soil and keep it shaded so the white does not scorch. Lavender flowers rise above the foliage in summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in dappled shade in fertile soil.

directions: This large hosta shows deep green leaves banded with a wide, rippling yellow margin that ages to cream. Plant the crown level with the soil in rich, moist ground and give it room to spread. Lavender flowers open on tall scapes in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a little morning sun deepens the gold.

directions: This large gold hosta forms a mound of thick, heavily puckered leaves with an almost metallic sheen. Plant the crown at grade in rich, moist soil and give a little morning light to intensify the color. Near-white flowers rise above the foliage in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade to keep the blue bloom intact.

directions: Famous for its deeply cupped, corrugated blue leaves that hold water like little bowls, this hosta is a collector's favorite. Plant the crown level with the soil in cool, moist ground and keep it shaded to protect the waxy blue coating. Near-white flowers appear in early summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in bright shade in fertile soil.

directions: This large hosta rings its blue-green, cupped leaves with a wide, clean white margin that reads like a halo. Plant the crown at grade in rich, moisture-holding soil. The heavy substance shrugs off slugs, and near-white flowers rise above the mound in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade to preserve the blue leaf color.

directions: A Tardiana hosta with dimpled, powder-blue leaves that form a rounded, symmetrical mound. Plant the crown level with the soil in cool, moist ground and keep it shaded to hold the blue. Pale lavender flowers open on upright scapes in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade for the best blue tones.

directions: This compact blue hosta forms a neat mound of heart-shaped, waxy leaves ideal for the middle of a shady border. Plant the crown at grade in cool, moist soil and keep out of hot sun. Lavender flowers rise on slender scapes in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in bright shade; blooms fragrantly in late season.

directions: A sport in the fragrant Guacamole family, this hosta shows shiny gold leaf centers framed by dark green margins. Plant the crown level with the soil in rich, moist ground. Large, sweetly scented near-white flowers open on tall scapes in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in bright shade to hold the vivid variegation.

directions: This bright hosta layers creamy-white and green streaked centers inside darker green margins for a festive look. Plant the crown at grade in rich, moisture-retentive soil. Lavender flowers rise above the mound in midsummer; give it filtered light to keep the colors sharp.

planting.note: Plant in spring; red petioles show best against gold leaves.

directions: This eye-catching hosta pairs golden leaves with striking red-purple petioles and flower scapes. Plant the crown level with the soil in rich, moist ground with a little morning light to deepen the gold. Lavender flowers on red-tinted stems appear in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in bright shade; needs room to reach full size.

directions: A large sport of Blue Angel, this hosta rings its big blue-green leaves with a soft creamy-yellow margin aging to white. Plant the crown at grade in deep, fertile, moist soil and allow generous space. Pale lavender flowers rise on tall scapes in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade to protect the white centers.

directions: A compact sport of Loyalist, this upright little hosta holds narrow leaves with bright white centers streaked into dark green margins. Plant the crown level with the soil in moist, humus-rich ground. Lavender flowers rise above the spiky mound in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade to hold the blue coloring.

directions: Unusual among blue hostas for its scented blooms, this compact mound holds heart-shaped, powder-blue leaves. Plant the crown at grade in cool, moist soil and keep out of hot sun to preserve the blue. Fragrant pale lavender flowers open on upright scapes in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in bright shade for strong center-to-margin contrast.

directions: This bright hosta sets glossy gold leaf centers inside wide, dark green margins for a dramatic spotlight effect. Plant the crown level with the soil in rich, moist ground. Near-white flowers rise above the shining mound in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in bright shade to deepen the gold.

directions: This small gold hosta forms a tight mound of thick, deeply cupped and puckered leaves. Plant the crown at grade in rich, moist soil with a little morning light to brighten the color. Near-white flowers appear on short scapes in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in spring; ideal for containers and small shady spots.

directions: A sport of Blue Mouse Ears, this miniature hosta holds thick, rounded blue-green leaves edged in creamy yellow. Plant the crown level with the soil in moist, well-drained ground or a container. Lavender flowers rise just above the little mound in early to midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade to keep the blue coloring rich.

directions: This striking blue hosta lifts long, narrow, deeply rippled leaves that arch outward like sea spray. Plant the crown at grade in cool, moist ground and keep it shaded to hold the powdery blue. Lavender flowers rise on tall scapes in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade; ideal for troughs and containers.

directions: This miniature hosta holds tiny leaves with creamy-white centers ringed by blue-green margins, perfect for a trough garden. Plant the crown level with the soil in moist, well-drained ground and grow it slowly with care. Lavender flowers rise on short scapes in midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in bright shade to reveal the leaf pattern.

directions: This novelty hosta imprints a darker green, maple-leaf-shaped pattern within its gold leaves like a tattoo. Plant the crown at grade in moist, humus-rich soil in bright shade to develop the design. Lavender flowers rise on short scapes in summer above the small mound.

planting.note: Plant in spring; a little morning sun deepens the gold and scent.

directions: This large gold hosta forms a big mound of thick, wavy leaves that curl at the edges like tortilla chips. Plant the crown level with the soil in rich, moist ground. Fragrant near-white flowers open on tall scapes in late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in shade to keep the blue-green and gold crisp.

directions: This upright hosta lifts narrow, lance-shaped blue-green leaves edged in golden cream that arch outward from the crown. Plant the crown at grade in moist, humus-rich soil. Lavender flowers rise on tall scapes in mid to late summer above the fountain-like mound.

planting.note: Divide and replant in late summer so roots settle before winter.

directions: Bury the rhizome so its top just shows at the soil surface in a sunny, well-drained bed. The soft-blue flowers carry a distinct grape or vanilla fragrance in late spring, and the gray-green foliage stays handsome afterward. Avoid mulching over the rhizomes, which can rot in wet, crowded conditions.

planting.note: Replant divisions in late summer in full sun for the brightest leaf color.

directions: Set the rhizome shallowly with its crown at soil level and give it lean, sharply drained ground. The cream-and-green striped fans are the main attraction, topped by fragrant lavender-blue flowers in late spring. Divide crowded clumps every few years to keep the variegation vigorous.

planting.note: Divide clumps after flowering or in early spring.

directions: Plant the shallow rhizomes in rich, moist but well-drained soil in light shade or morning sun. Flat, crested lilac-blue flowers with darker veining open in late spring above fans of ribbed leaves. Refresh with fresh soil and division every few years, as old clumps decline quickly.

planting.note: Plant against a warm wall in late summer or early fall and disturb as little as possible.

directions: Site the rhizomes in poor, sharply drained soil at the base of a sunny wall where they can bake in summer. Fragrant lavender flowers appear at ground level through winter and early spring, nestled among grassy evergreen leaves. Pick blooms in bud and let them open indoors to enjoy them out of the cold.

planting.note: Transplant in early fall while roots are active and keep disturbance minimal.

directions: Plant divisions about two inches deep in deep, fertile soil and water well through spring, then allow a drier summer rest. Orchid-like flowers in blue, yellow, or bronze rise on tall stems in early summer, well after the bearded irises finish. Clumps resent being moved, so choose a permanent spot and leave them alone.

planting.note: Set divisions in fall in deep, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Give this stately spuria-type iris rich soil and steady spring moisture, tapering off as summer heat arrives. Crisp white flowers with a bold yellow blaze open on tall stems in early summer. Established clumps are long-lived and best left undivided for many years.

planting.note: Divide and replant in spring in moist ground.

directions: Plant divisions in consistently moist, humus-rich soil in sun or light shade. This very cold-hardy species bears purple-blue flowers with reduced upright petals, giving them a distinctive open, pinwheel look in early summer. It tolerates boggy sites and short growing seasons well.

planting.note: Set divisions in wet ground or shallow water in spring.

directions: Plant along stream banks, pond margins, or in rain gardens where soil stays wet. Soft blue-violet flowers with a yellow signal open in late spring to early summer above arching foliage. It naturalizes readily in boggy ground and suits native and wetland plantings across the Southeast.

planting.note: Divide in spring where soil is moist in early season and drier by summer.

directions: This Western native likes soil that is wet in spring and dries out over summer, mimicking mountain meadows. Pale lavender-blue flowers veined with darker lines appear in late spring on slender stems. Give it full sun and avoid heavy summer watering once bloom has finished.

planting.note: Divide and plant in fall as cool, moist weather returns.

directions: Plant this Pacific Coast native in fall in lean, well-drained soil under light shade or coastal sun. Flowers in shades of lavender, cream, and deep purple open in spring above tough, evergreen leaf fans. Divide only when clumps are actively rooting in autumn, as summer transplanting often fails.

planting.note: Move divisions in fall while roots are growing.

directions: Grow this compact Northwestern native in well-drained soil with dry summers, in sun or dappled shade. Slender flowers in purple, lavender, or occasionally white and yellow open in mid to late spring among fine grassy leaves. It tolerates drought once established and dislikes rich, soggy ground.

planting.note: Divide in fall into well-drained, humus-rich soil.

directions: Plant this dainty Pacific Coast species in light shade with acidic, freely draining soil and cool summers. Flowers range from golden yellow through apricot to lavender and open in late spring over neat grassy tufts. It is a key parent of many hybrid Pacifica irises grown along the West Coast.

planting.note: Divide in spring into rich, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Grow in deep, damp, humus-rich soil in sun or light shade where roots never dry out. The velvety wine-black flowers, marked with fine gold veins, open in early summer above narrow grassy leaves. Keep clumps well watered and divide every few years to maintain vigor.

planting.note: Divide in spring or early fall into moist soil.

directions: Closely related to the Siberian iris, this species thrives in moist, fertile soil in full sun to light shade. Rich violet-blue flowers open in late spring on stems held just above the grassy foliage. It forms tidy, non-invasive clumps and is a parent of many garden Siberian hybrids.

planting.note: Divide and replant in late summer in sharply drained soil.

directions: Plant the small rhizomes just at the surface in gritty, well-drained soil at the front of a border or rock garden. Stemless flowers in purple, yellow, cream, or blue hug the ground in early spring, weeks ahead of the tall bearded irises. Keep the crowns unmulched and sunny to prevent rot.

planting.note: Replant divisions in late summer with the rhizome near the surface.

directions: Give this old species iris lean, well-drained soil and full sun with its rhizome barely covered. Bicolored flowers with yellow standards and brown-veined falls open in late spring, a color pattern behind many modern variegata-type hybrids. Divide crowded clumps in late summer to keep them blooming freely.

planting.note: Set divisions in fall in ordinary garden soil.

directions: Plant in ordinary, reasonably moist soil in sun to light shade where the low flowers can be appreciated up close. The violet-and-purple blooms are partly hidden among grassy leaves but carry a striking scent of ripe plums or greengages. Leave clumps undisturbed to build up over several seasons.

planting.note: Divide in spring; tolerant of poor, dry, and alkaline soils.

directions: This tough steppe species handles drought, salt, and poor alkaline soils that defeat most irises. Pale bluish-white flowers appear in late spring above stiff, upright grassy foliage. Plant in full sun and give little supplemental care once established.

planting.note: Divide and replant in mid to late summer, at least six weeks before frost.

directions: Set the rhizome horizontally with its top exposed to the sun in well-drained, neutral soil. Fragrant purple flowers with paler falls open in late spring, the classic old-garden bearded iris behind countless hybrids. Lift and divide crowded clumps every three to four years right after bloom.

planting.note: Divide in late summer into gritty, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant the small rhizomes shallowly in sharply drained, gritty soil in full sun. Fragrant flowers in yellow, violet, or bicolor forms open in early to mid spring on short stems. This tough dwarf species suits rock gardens and dry sunny banks and dislikes wet winter soil.

planting.note: Plant in late summer in very well-drained soil that bakes in summer.

directions: Give this Central Asian Regelia iris a hot, dry summer bake and sharply drained, gritty soil in full sun. Smooth, satiny flowers in soft lavender-blue with a faint fragrance open in late spring. Protect it from wet winters, and lift or cover the rhizomes in regions with cold, damp soil.

planting.note: Divide after flowering into moist, humus-rich woodland soil.

directions: Plant this delicate woodland iris in cool, moist, leafy soil in dappled to partial shade. Airy lilac flowers with a small white-and-orange crest float on wiry, branching stems in late spring. It spreads slowly into a graceful groundcover under shrubs and does not tolerate hot, dry conditions.

planting.note: Divide and replant in mid to late summer, rhizome top exposed.

directions: Set the rhizome just at soil level in a sunny, well-drained bed and keep mulch off the crown. This award-winning plicata bears crisp white flowers stitched and rimmed in deep violet in late spring. Cut back the fans after bloom and divide crowded clumps every few years to keep flowering strong.

planting.note: Replant divisions in late summer in full sun.

directions: Plant the rhizome shallowly in a sunny, freely draining spot and water sparingly once established. This classic older variety shows white standards above rich violet-purple falls edged in white, blooming in late spring. Its historic amoena coloring made it a landmark iris still grown today.

planting.note: Divide and set out in late summer with the rhizome at the surface.

directions: Give the rhizome full sun and sharp drainage, keeping its top just visible above the soil. Large, ruffled medium-blue flowers with a white blaze open on tall, well-branched stems in late spring. Deadhead spent blooms and divide clumps every few years to sustain the display.

planting.note: Replant in late summer in a sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: Plant with the rhizome barely covered in fertile, freely draining soil and full sun. Big, heavily ruffled flowers in deep violet-blue open on strong stems in late spring. Keep the crown dry and unmulched, and lift crowded clumps after bloom to divide.

planting.note: Divide and replant in late summer, rhizome top exposed to sun.

directions: Set the rhizome at the surface in a hot, sunny, well-drained bed. Nearly black, silky flowers in deep burgundy-purple open in late spring and read almost coal-dark in strong light. Give it good air movement and lean soil, and divide every few years to keep it thriving.

planting.note: Replant divisions in late summer in full sun.

directions: Plant with the rhizome just at soil level in a sunny, well-drained spot. This distinctive variety bears pale greenish-tan flowers with long, projecting horns on the beards in late spring. Its unusual color and spiky beards make it a collector favorite; divide crowded clumps after flowering.

planting.note: Divide and replant in late summer, keeping the rhizome shallow.

directions: Give the rhizome full sun and sharp drainage with its top exposed. Large, ruffled flowers in shimmering silvery-blue open on tall, sturdy stems in late spring. Remove spent bloom stalks at the base and divide every few years to maintain vigor.

planting.note: Replant in late summer in a sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: Set the rhizome at the surface in full sun with excellent drainage. Soft powder-blue, ruffled flowers with a sweet fragrance open in late spring on well-branched stems. A vigorous, reliable rebloomer in mild climates; divide crowded clumps after the main flush.

planting.note: Divide and replant in late summer with the rhizome top exposed.

directions: Plant shallowly in a sunny, freely draining bed and avoid burying the crown. Soft pink flowers with tangerine beards open in late spring and remain a benchmark self-pink iris. Deadhead after bloom and lift congested clumps every few years to divide.

planting.note: Replant divisions in late summer in full sun.

directions: Set the rhizome just at soil level in a sunny, well-drained spot. Large, crisply ruffled pure-white flowers open on tall stems in late spring. Keep the crown unmulched and dry, and divide crowded clumps after bloom to maintain flowering.

planting.note: Divide and set out in late summer, rhizome barely covered.

directions: Plant with the rhizome at the surface in full sun and sharp drainage. Striking bicolor flowers pair golden-yellow standards with velvety burgundy-red falls in late spring. It grows tall and vigorous; stake in exposed sites and divide crowded clumps after flowering.

planting.note: Replant divisions in late summer in full sun.

directions: Set the rhizome at soil level in a sunny, well-drained bed. This award-winning plicata bears white flowers boldly banded and stippled in violet-purple in late spring. Deadhead spent stalks and divide congested clumps every few years to keep it blooming freely.

planting.note: Divide and replant in late summer, keeping the rhizome shallow.

directions: Plant with the rhizome top exposed in full sun and freely draining soil. Warm golden-bronze and copper-toned flowers open on strong stems in late spring. Keep the crown dry, remove spent bloom stalks, and divide crowded clumps after flowering.

planting.note: Replant divisions in late summer in a sunny bed.

directions: Set the rhizome just at the surface in full sun and sharp drainage. Rich, saturated violet-blue flowers with heavy ruffling open on tall stems in late spring. Deadhead after bloom and divide every few years to sustain the deep color and vigor.

planting.note: Divide and replant in late summer with the rhizome exposed.

directions: Plant shallowly in full sun and well-drained soil. This striking variety shows white flowers with falls neatly rimmed in a band of blue-violet, blooming in late spring. Keep the crown unmulched and dry, and divide crowded clumps after the main flush.

planting.note: Replant divisions in late summer in full sun.

directions: Set the rhizome at soil level in a sunny, freely draining bed. Bicolor flowers combine soft lavender-pink standards with wine-purple-edged falls in late spring. A reliable rebloomer in warm regions; deadhead and divide crowded clumps every few years.

planting.note: Divide and set out in late summer, rhizome barely covered.

directions: Plant with the rhizome top exposed in full sun and sharp drainage. Large, ruffled flowers glow in warm coral-rose with tangerine beards in late spring. Remove spent stalks and divide congested clumps after bloom to keep the display strong.

planting.note: Replant divisions in late summer, keeping the rhizome shallow.

directions: Set the rhizome just at the surface in a hot, sunny, well-drained bed. Deep blackish-purple flowers with a satiny sheen open on tall stems in late spring. Give it strong light to bring out the near-black tone, and divide crowded clumps after flowering.

planting.note: Divide and replant in late summer, rhizome top exposed.

directions: Plant the rhizome shallowly in full sun with excellent drainage. This popular plicata bears white flowers crisply stitched and rimmed in violet-purple, on compact stems in late spring. Its smaller stature suits the front of the border; divide crowded clumps after bloom.

planting.note: Replant divisions in late summer to establish before winter.

directions: Set the rhizome at soil level in full sun and well-drained, fertile soil, and feed lightly to fuel rebloom. Deep violet-purple flowers open in late spring and often return again in late summer or fall in warm zones. Water and feed after the first flush to encourage the second round of bloom.

planting.note: Set the divided root in early fall so the eyes settle before hard freeze.

directions: Plant the bare-root division with its pink buds no more than one and a half inches below the surface, since deeper eyes refuse to bloom. This early-season hybrid opens deep semi-double maroon-red cups on strong stems that rarely need staking. Cut foliage to the ground after autumn frost to reduce disease carryover.

planting.note: Fall division gives roots weeks to anchor before the ground locks up.

directions: Choose a sunny bed with sharp drainage and keep the eyes barely covered. The crepe-textured single white petals surround a golden boss of stamens in midseason and stand up well to rain. Divide only every eight to ten years, as this cultivar sulks when disturbed too often.

planting.note: Autumn is the ideal window for lifting and replanting roots.

directions: Work compost into the planting hole but keep fresh manure away from the crown. Broad single shell-pink blooms with contrasting yellow centers appear at midseason on tall, self-supporting stems. Deadhead spent flowers to keep the clump tidy and let the handsome foliage carry the border through summer.

planting.note: Plant in fall so the crown is established for spring growth.

directions: Site this early bloomer where morning sun dries the dew quickly to discourage botrytis. Semi-double flowers of glowing bright pink open on sturdy stems well ahead of most garden peonies. A light spring feeding of balanced fertilizer once shoots emerge supports the heavy first flush.

planting.note: Divide and replant in early autumn for best establishment.

directions: Keep the roots shallow and the site sunny for the strongest flower count. Each bloom pairs rose-cerise guard petals with a puff of creamy-white central petaloids for a striking two-tone effect in mid to late season. Support the stems with a grow-through ring, as the showy heads can flop after a downpour.

planting.note: Set roots in fall with eyes just under the soil line.

directions: Give this vigorous grower rich, well-drained loam and full sun. Large fragrant double crimson bombs open in the early-to-mid season and make excellent long-lasting cut flowers. Ring the clump early in spring so the substantial blooms stay upright through wind and rain.

planting.note: Fall planting lets the crown settle before winter dormancy.

directions: Plant with the buds no deeper than an inch and a half in a bed that never stays wet. This late-season award winner carries fully double blush-pink globes flecked faintly with crimson on strong stems. Its long stems and near-perfect form make it a favorite for the vase.

planting.note: Early autumn division gives the best root establishment.

directions: Choose full sun and keep the shallow-set eyes free of heavy mulch. Midseason double blooms open pale blush and fade to soft ivory-white, releasing a gentle sweet fragrance. Remove faded flowers and leave the foliage until frost to feed next year's buds.

planting.note: Plant roots in fall for spring flowering strength.

directions: Amend the planting hole with compost and keep the crown at the soil surface. Late-season fully double flowers open blush-pink and mellow toward cream, carrying a rich rose fragrance. Water deeply during dry spring weather to size up the buds, then let the plant coast through summer.

planting.note: Fall is the correct time to set or move the roots.

directions: Give it full sun and a well-drained site, keeping the eyes only lightly buried. The fragrant midseason blooms show creamy-white outer petals cradling a canary-yellow center that reads as a soft custard glow. Deadhead after flowering and leave the leaves standing to recharge the crown.

planting.note: Plant in early fall with the buds near the surface.

directions: This compact grower fits smaller borders and needs full sun to bloom heavily. Large midseason bomb-type flowers open soft rose-pink and pale toward the edges with a hint of ivory. Its shorter stems hold the ruffled heads upright with little or no staking.

planting.note: Autumn planting establishes roots ahead of winter.

directions: Set the divided root shallow in fertile, well-drained soil and full sun. Midseason bomb-form blooms surround white guard petals with a dense mound of buttery-yellow petaloids. Provide a support ring in spring so the heavy heads clear the ground after rain.

planting.note: Fall planting suits this vigorous cultivar best.

directions: Plant in deep, rich soil with the eyes an inch below grade for reliable bud set. The large mid-to-late-season bombs stack ivory guard petals, a ring of pale-yellow inner petals, and a rose-pink crown. Its tall stems benefit from early staking to carry the substantial flowers.

planting.note: Set the crown shallow in fall for best results.

directions: Give this late bloomer full sun to extend the peony season in the border. Rich rose-pink double flowers hold their saturated color well and finish the display after most cultivars fade. Cut the foliage to the ground in autumn and clear debris to limit fungal problems.

planting.note: Plant the division in fall with shallow-set eyes.

directions: This vigorous hybrid wants full sun and steady spring moisture to build its big flowers. Rose-form double blooms open a warm salmon-pink at midseason and soften as they age. Its strong stems and clean foliage make it a dependable choice for cutting gardens.

planting.note: Fall division and planting is preferred.

directions: Plant in full sun and free-draining soil, keeping the buds barely under the surface. Large fragrant double flowers open blush and mature to pure white at midseason. Deadhead spent blooms and support the tall stems to keep the heavy heads presentable.

planting.note: Plant in fall so roots establish before spring.

directions: Site this cultivar in full sun with the crown just below grade. Mid-to-late-season double flowers glow a deep ruby-red, some petals edged with a fine silver line. Feed lightly in early spring and stake the stems to display the rich color at its best.

planting.note: Autumn planting is best for this old French variety.

directions: Give this heritage cultivar full sun and shallow planting for dependable flowering. Large fragrant double blooms open pale rose-pink, sometimes marked with fine crimson flecks near the center, at midseason. Its long stems suit the vase, so cut in the soft-marshmallow bud stage for the longest life.

planting.note: Divide and plant in early autumn.

directions: Plant in full sun and rich, well-drained soil with the eyes near the surface. Fragrant midseason flowers open pale blush-pink and lighten toward the petal edges, often varying from double to semi-double on the same plant. Leave the foliage intact until frost to build strong buds for the following year.

planting.note: Set roots in fall for spring establishment.

directions: Give full sun and shallow planting to this dark-flowered classic. Midseason double blooms open one of the deepest maroon-reds among peonies, glowing almost black-red in bud. Site it against a light backdrop so the rich color reads clearly from a distance.

planting.note: Autumn planting gives reliable establishment.

directions: Plant this popular cut-flower variety in full sun with the buds just under the soil. Large fragrant double flowers of clear medium pink open at midseason on long, strong stems. Ring the clump in spring so a rain shower does not tip the full heads to the ground.

planting.note: Plant the hybrid root in fall, eyes shallow.

directions: This robust hybrid wants full sun and deep, fertile soil to fuel its large flowers. Enormous semi-double to double blooms of brilliant red open at midseason on notably strong stems. Its sturdy habit means it often stands without staking even in exposed spots.

planting.note: Plant in early fall for this early-blooming hybrid.

directions: This compact Saunders hybrid blooms early and suits the front of a sunny border. Cupped semi-double flowers glow a bright coral-rose and hold their form well on strong, self-supporting stems. Keep the eyes shallow and provide sharp drainage to keep the crown healthy.

planting.note: Plant the division in fall with shallow eyes.

directions: Give this early hybrid full sun to bring out its warm coloring. Semi-double flowers open a soft salmon-pink and gradually fade to a paler blush as they mature. Its tidy habit and strong stems make it a good choice for smaller sunny beds.

planting.note: Plant in fall with the crown near the surface.

directions: Plant this award-winning hybrid in full sun with shallow-set eyes. Japanese-form flowers pair dark red guard petals with a center tuft of gold-edged staminodes for a striking contrast at midseason. Its strong stems seldom flop, making staking optional.

planting.note: Plant in fall for early-season bloom the following years.

directions: This vigorous hybrid needs full sun and rich soil to support its huge flowers. Very large double blooms of glowing deep red open early on tall, strong stems. Provide a grow-through ring in spring, as even sturdy stems can bend under the enormous heads.

planting.note: Plant in fall for reliable early bloom.

directions: Give this early hybrid full sun to open its wide flowers on schedule. Large single fiery-red blooms display a bright golden center of stamens and stand tall on strong stems. The single form sheds rain quickly, so it rarely needs support.

planting.note: Plant in early fall for this early Saunders hybrid.

directions: This early Saunders hybrid opens among the first peonies of the season in full sun. Large single flowers open pale blush with faint lavender tones and fade to near-white with a golden center. Keep the shallow eyes free of heavy mulch and enjoy the airy, early display.

planting.note: Plant in fall for the earliest spring flowers.

directions: This very early Saunders hybrid blooms well ahead of the main peony season in full sun. Single flowers of soft rose-pink open above finely divided foliage on a compact, tidy plant. Its early habit and delicate form suit the front of a sunny border.

planting.note: Plant in fall; this fernleaf hybrid blooms very early.

directions: This fernleaf-type hybrid opens some of the earliest peony flowers of spring in full sun. Single cup-shaped blooms of deep red rise above finely cut, feathery green foliage on a low, mounding plant. Give it sharp drainage and full sun, and let the fine foliage remain as a soft textural accent after bloom.

planting.note: Plant in fall for early-to-mid-season bloom.

directions: This award-winning hybrid opens brilliant single flowers in full sun early in the season. Wide scarlet-red blooms surround a bright cluster of golden stamens and hold their color without fading badly. Its strong stems and single form shed rain well and rarely need staking.

planting.note: Plant in fall with eyes near the surface.

directions: Plant this graceful old French variety in full sun with the crown just below grade. Single to Japanese-form flowers of soft pink surround a center of golden staminodes at midseason. The light, open blooms give an airy look and attract bees to the sunny border.

planting.note: Plant in fall for spring establishment.

directions: Give this striking Japanese-form variety full sun and shallow planting. Each midseason flower sets a mound of creamy-white central petaloids against a ring of deep raspberry-red guard petals. The bold two-tone contrast makes it a standout in a mixed border.

planting.note: Plant in fall with the eyes just under the soil.

directions: Plant in full sun with sharp drainage and keep the crown shallow. Anemone-form midseason flowers pair rose-cerise guard petals with a raised cushion of soft pink central petaloids. Its neat habit and strong stems make it a reliable cut flower for arrangements.

planting.note: Divide and plant in early fall.

directions: Set this fragrant variety in full sun with the buds barely covered. Large fully double flowers of soft blush-pink open at midseason and fade toward pale cream. Its strong stems hold the full heads well, though a support ring helps after heavy rain.

planting.note: Plant this early variety in fall.

directions: This fragrant heritage variety blooms early in full sun. Full double bomb-type flowers open blush and mature to white, with faint carmine touches at the heart. Cut in the soft bud stage for the vase to enjoy the rich scent indoors.

planting.note: Plant the Itoh division in fall with the eyes about two inches deep.

directions: This intersectional hybrid combines a herbaceous habit with the strong stems and cut foliage of a tree peony. Semi-double flowers open a soft canary-yellow flushed with apricot and pink, then pale as they age, over a long midseason window. Leave the sturdy stems standing and cut the plant back near the ground after autumn frost.

planting.note: Plant in fall with the eyes roughly two inches below grade.

directions: This vigorous Itoh forms a wide, well-branched mound that needs no staking. Semi-double flowers open lavender-pink with darker rose flares at the base and fade gently over a long bloom period. Give it full sun and room to spread, and clear the foliage after frost.

planting.note: Plant in fall, setting the eyes about two inches deep.

directions: This large intersectional hybrid produces an abundance of blooms over several weeks. Semi-double flowers open a warm apricot-tan with rosy flares near the center and shift color as they mature. Plant in full sun with good drainage and let the strong stems support the heavy flowers on their own.

planting.note: Plant in fall with the eyes about two inches below the surface.

directions: This intersectional hybrid opens single, wide-cupped flowers of vivid scarlet-red with a golden center. The bright blooms appear over a long midseason period above deeply cut, tree-peony-like foliage. Give it full sun and sharp drainage, and cut the sturdy stems back to the ground after frost.

planting.note: Set tubers out only once the soil has warmed and frost has passed.

directions: Plant the tuber horizontally about 4 to 6 inches deep with the growing eye pointing upward. Stake early and pinch the growing tip once to encourage more of the soft rose-pink waterlily blooms. Keep the soil evenly moist through summer and cut flowers freely to extend the display.

planting.note: Plant after the last spring frost when the ground is workable and warm.

directions: Bury the tuber 4 to 6 inches deep in fertile, free-draining soil and drive in a support stake at planting time. This reliable yellow waterlily form flowers heavily from midsummer, so deadhead spent blooms to keep new buds coming. Lift and store the tubers frost-free over winter in cold zones.

planting.note: Wait until soil temperatures are reliably warm before planting the tuber.

directions: Set the tuber about 5 inches deep with its neck near the surface and stake as growth begins. The silvery-pink waterlily blooms are excellent for cutting, so harvest stems in the cool of the morning when flowers are three-quarters open. Feed with a high-potash fertilizer through the flowering season.

planting.note: Plant tubers outdoors once all danger of frost has passed.

directions: Plant the tuber 4 to 6 inches deep in a sunny bed and stake the stems before they get tall. The glowing ruby-red waterlily flowers appear from midsummer into fall and hold well in the vase. Pinch the leading shoot early to build a bushier, more productive plant.

planting.note: Delay planting until the soil has warmed after the last frost.

directions: Lay the tuber 4 to 6 inches deep in rich, well-drained soil and place a stake at the same time to avoid spearing roots later. The soft cream-and-blush waterlily blooms are prized by cut-flower growers and appear over a long summer season. Water deeply during dry spells and deadhead regularly.

planting.note: Plant out after frost danger passes and the soil is warm.

directions: Set the tuber about 5 inches deep and provide a sturdy stake for the long, strong cutting stems this Karma variety is bred for. The warm orange waterlily blooms last exceptionally well once cut, so harvest when nearly fully open. Keep plants fed and watered to sustain flowering into autumn.

planting.note: Plant tubers once the risk of frost has ended.

directions: Plant the tuber horizontally 4 to 6 inches deep and stake early for cutting. This Karma selection carries warm pink-and-peach waterlily blooms on long stems ideal for the vase. Deadhead spent flowers and feed weekly with a potassium-rich feed for continuous bloom.

planting.note: Plant after the last frost when the soil has warmed.

directions: Bury the tuber 4 to 6 inches deep and stake the plant as it grows tall. The deep velvety maroon-red semi-cactus blooms, with their rolled, pointed petals, appear from midsummer until frost. Cut flowers regularly and remove faded heads to keep the plant producing.

planting.note: Set tubers out only after the danger of frost has fully passed.

directions: Plant the tuber about 5 inches deep in a warm, sunny position and stake before the stems lengthen. The near-black burgundy semi-cactus flowers have narrow, quilled petals that make dramatic cut stems. Pinch the tip early and feed regularly to encourage a long flush of blooms.

planting.note: Plant once frost has passed; this compact type suits containers too.

directions: Plant the tuber 4 to 5 inches deep in a bed or large pot, as this is a dwarf cactus type that rarely needs staking. Soft pink spiky blooms are carried freely from summer into autumn on tidy, compact plants. Deadhead often and water container-grown plants regularly to keep flowering strong.

planting.note: Plant tubers out after the final frost of spring.

directions: Set the tuber 4 to 6 inches deep and support the stems with a stake as they grow. The rich scarlet cactus blooms with rolled, pointed petals are long lasting and good for cutting. Pinch out the growing point when young and remove spent flowers to prolong the season.

planting.note: Wait for warm soil after the last frost before planting.

directions: Plant the tuber about 5 inches deep in fertile soil and stake early. The pure white semi-cactus flowers on long stems are a florist's favorite and cut beautifully. Feed with a high-potash fertilizer and deadhead spent blooms to keep the plant producing until frost.

planting.note: Plant outdoors after all frost danger has passed.

directions: Plant the tuber 5 to 6 inches deep and stake this tall variety firmly to carry its large blooms. The bright clear-yellow semi-cactus flowers are held on strong stems and are a proven show and cutting variety. Disbud side buds for the biggest flowers and keep the soil evenly moist.

planting.note: Plant tubers after the last frost when soil has warmed.

directions: Set the tuber 5 to 6 inches deep and stake this vigorous plant early to support its spiky blooms. The rich purple cactus flowers have narrow, incurving petals and are popular on the show bench. Pinch to encourage branching and disbud for larger, cleaner blooms.

planting.note: Plant out once the soil is warm and frost has passed.

directions: Plant the tuber about 5 inches deep and support the stems as they grow. This small cactus variety carries blends of yellow flushed with red on tidy plants and is a reliable exhibition and cutting flower. Deadhead often and feed through summer to keep the blooms coming.

planting.note: Plant after frost when the soil has warmed through.

directions: Set the tuber 4 to 6 inches deep in full sun and stake the stems early. Fiery orange-and-yellow semi-cactus blooms, whose name means firebird, glow through the summer garden. Pinch the leader once, deadhead spent flowers, and lift the tubers before hard frost in cold regions.

planting.note: Plant tubers out after the last spring frost.

directions: Plant the tuber 4 to 6 inches deep and provide a stake as growth develops. The warm golden-orange semi-cactus blooms are freely produced and useful for cutting. Keep the soil evenly moist, feed through the season, and remove faded heads to encourage new buds.

planting.note: Plant out only when the soil has warmed after frost.

directions: Plant the tuber 5 to 6 inches deep and stake the tall stems firmly. This large incurved cactus type has slender, twisting petals in white edged with rose-purple that create a spidery look. Disbud for the biggest flowers and cut stems when the blooms are nearly fully open.

planting.note: Plant after the last frost when the ground is warm.

directions: Set the tuber 4 to 6 inches deep and stake early to hold the stems upright. The neat, rounded burnt-orange ball blooms are packed with rolled florets and are outstanding for cutting. Deadhead regularly and feed with a potassium-rich fertilizer to keep flowers coming.

planting.note: Plant tubers out after all frost has passed.

directions: Plant the tuber about 5 inches deep and support the stems with a stake. The deep wine-red ball blooms are neatly formed and long lasting, making them a top choice for wedding and autumn arrangements. Cut freely and remove faded heads to keep new buds forming.

planting.note: Plant out when the soil warms after the final frost.

directions: Bury the tuber 4 to 6 inches deep and stake the plant early. The small rich-purple ball blooms are densely packed and hold their color and shape well when cut. Pinch the growing tip once, deadhead often, and lift the tubers for winter storage in cold areas.

planting.note: Plant tubers after the last frost of spring.

directions: Set the tuber 4 to 6 inches deep in fertile soil and stake early. The crisp white ball blooms are perfectly formed and prized by cut-flower growers for late-season arrangements. Deadhead regularly and feed with a high-potash fertilizer to extend flowering into autumn.

planting.note: Plant out after the last frost when soil is warm.

directions: Plant the tuber about 5 inches deep and provide a stake early. The intense dark-red ball blooms are tightly formed and hold well both in the garden and the vase. Pinch to build a bushy plant and deadhead spent flowers to keep the display going.

planting.note: Plant tubers out once frost danger has passed.

directions: Set the tuber 4 to 6 inches deep and stake as growth begins. The soft pink miniature ball blooms have a honeycomb pattern of rolled florets and are excellent for cutting. Deadhead often and feed through the summer to keep the plant flowering freely.

planting.note: Plant after the last frost when the ground has warmed.

directions: Plant the tuber 4 to 6 inches deep and stake the tall stems. The large ball blooms are streaked and speckled purple over white, so no two flowers look quite alike. Cut regularly, deadhead, and feed to keep the flush of marbled blooms going through fall.

planting.note: Plant tubers out after the danger of frost has passed.

directions: Set the tuber about 5 inches deep and stake early. The rounded pink ball blooms are neatly formed and hold their shape superbly, making them a dependable cut flower. Pinch the leader once, water in dry spells, and remove spent heads to prolong flowering.

planting.note: Plant out only after the last frost when soil is warm.

directions: Plant the tuber 5 to 6 inches deep and stake as the plant grows. The warm bronze-and-yellow semi-cactus blooms are held on strong stems and perform well both in borders and on the show bench. Disbud for larger flowers and keep well watered through summer.

planting.note: Plant tubers out after the last spring frost.

directions: Set the tuber 4 to 6 inches deep and stake as needed. The small, perfectly spherical pompon blooms in lilac-pink are made of tightly rolled florets and appear in great numbers. Deadhead often to keep the plant tidy and productive right up to frost.

planting.note: Plant out when soil is warm after the last frost.

directions: Plant the tuber about 5 inches deep and stake the stems. This variety produces masses of tiny, globe-shaped white pompon blooms that are a favorite for exhibition and cutting. Pinch the growing tip early and deadhead regularly for a long flowering season.

planting.note: Plant tubers out after all frost danger has passed.

directions: Set the tuber 4 to 6 inches deep and stake early. The small, rounded royal-purple pompon blooms are neatly formed and produced in abundance on wiry stems. Deadhead often and feed through summer to keep the plant flowering into autumn.

planting.note: Plant out after the last frost when soil is warm.

directions: Plant the tuber 4 to 6 inches deep and provide light staking. The tiny violet pompon blooms are tightly rolled and globe-shaped, prized for both showing and small arrangements. Pinch the tip once and deadhead regularly to encourage a continuous flush.

planting.note: Plant tubers out after the last frost of spring.

directions: Set the tuber 4 to 6 inches deep and stake as it grows. The rounded red-purple ball blooms are compact and long-lasting, useful for both borders and cutting. Deadhead often, feed with a potassium-rich fertilizer, and lift the tubers before hard frost in cold zones.

planting.note: Plant out when soil is warm after the final frost.

directions: Plant the tuber 4 to 6 inches deep and stake lightly. The small lavender-lilac pompon blooms are tightly formed globes carried on slim, upright stems. Pinch the leading shoot early and deadhead throughout the season to keep flowers coming.

planting.note: Plant tubers out once the soil is warm and frost has passed.

directions: Plant the tuber 6 inches deep and drive in a strong stake to carry the giant, dinnerplate-sized blooms. The huge pale-yellow decorative flowers can span ten inches or more when disbudded to one bud per stem. Water and feed generously and tie in the heavy stems as they lengthen.

planting.note: Plant after the last frost when the ground has warmed.

directions: Set the tuber about 6 inches deep and stake this tall, big-flowered variety firmly. The enormous rich burgundy-red decorative blooms are a striking dinnerplate type that commands attention. Disbud for the largest flowers, feed and water well, and support the heavy stems.

planting.note: Plant out only after the danger of frost has passed.

directions: Plant the tuber 6 inches deep and stake strongly. The large golden-bronze decorative blooms are dinnerplate-sized and a proven exhibition variety. Disbud side buds for the biggest flowers, keep the plant well fed and watered, and cut for long-lasting arrangements.

planting.note: Plant tubers out after the last frost when soil is warm.

directions: Set the tuber about 6 inches deep and provide a stout stake. The huge deep-red decorative blooms are a classic dinnerplate dahlia that makes a bold garden statement. Disbud for maximum size, water deeply in dry weather, and tie stems in as they grow.

planting.note: Plant out after frost danger passes and the soil warms.

directions: Plant the tuber about 5 inches deep and stake early for cutting. This Karma variety carries decorative blooms in blends of pink, coral and cream on long, strong stems bred for the vase. Harvest when nearly fully open, deadhead spent flowers, and feed through summer.

planting.note: Plant tubers out when the soil is warm after frost.

directions: Set the tuber 5 inches deep and stake for support. This Karma cut-flower variety bears deep wine-red decorative blooms with near-black foliage on long, sturdy stems. Cut when flowers are nearly open, remove faded heads, and keep the plant fed and watered.

planting.note: Plant out after the last spring frost.

directions: Plant the tuber 5 to 6 inches deep and stake the tall stems. The large decorative blooms are white boldly striped and flecked with rich pink-purple, so each flower is unique. Disbud for bigger blooms, deadhead spent heads, and keep the soil evenly moist.

planting.note: Plant out after frost; this dwarf type is ideal for pots.

directions: Plant the tuber 3 to 4 inches deep in a pot or the front of a border, as this dwarf decorative rarely needs staking. Warm salmon-pink blooms are produced generously on compact, bushy plants all summer. Deadhead often and water container plants regularly to keep them flowering.

planting.note: Plant tubers out after the last frost when soil is warm.

directions: Set the tuber 6 inches deep and stake this tall variety firmly. The large decorative blooms blend fiery red and golden-yellow like a sunset and make a bold cut flower. Disbud for size, feed and water generously, and tie in the stems as the plant grows.

planting.note: Plant out after the last frost of spring.

directions: Plant the tuber 4 inches deep in full sun; this dwarf single rarely needs staking. Golden-yellow single blooms with a dark eye sit above bronze-tinged foliage and are much loved by bees. Deadhead to prolong flowering and enjoy the pollinator-friendly blooms until frost.

planting.note: Plant out after all frost danger has passed.

directions: Plant the tuber 4 to 5 inches deep and stake lightly if needed. Bright magenta-pink single blooms with a golden center are set off against dark, near-black foliage and draw in pollinators. Deadhead regularly to keep the plant producing its open, bee-friendly flowers.

planting.note: Plant tubers out once the soil is warm after frost.

directions: Plant the tuber 4 inches deep; this compact, dark-leaved variety usually needs no staking. Rich red peony-form blooms with a small cluster of central florets glow against bronze-black foliage. Deadhead often and grow in a pot or the front of a sunny border for best effect.

planting.note: Plant out after the last frost when soil has warmed.

directions: Set the tuber 4 to 5 inches deep and stake lightly. Semi-double peony-form blooms in warm coral-pink with fluted petals are carried above dark bronze foliage and attract bees. Pinch the tip early for a bushier plant and deadhead to extend the flowering season.

planting.note: Plant out after the last frost when the ground is warm.

directions: Plant the tuber 4 to 6 inches deep and stake as it grows. Each anemone-form flower has a ring of orange outer petals around a raised, pincushion center of tangerine tufts, and pollinators love them. Deadhead often to keep the long succession of blooms coming until frost.

planting.note: Plant tubers out after the last spring frost.

directions: Plant the tuber 4 to 6 inches deep and stake as needed. Soft yellow single outer petals surround a frilled inner collar and a golden center, making an open flower much visited by bees. Deadhead regularly to keep the plant blooming steadily through the season.

planting.note: Plant out once frost has passed and the soil is warm.

directions: Set the tuber 4 to 6 inches deep and stake the stems. Deep maroon-red single outer petals surround a creamy-yellow inner collar, giving a striking two-tone collarette flower that pollinators favor. Deadhead often to keep new buds forming right up to the first frost.

planting.note: Plant out after the last frost when soil has warmed.

directions: Plant the tuber 5 to 6 inches deep and stake the tall stems. The deep velvety maroon-red decorative blooms are richly colored and make dramatic cut flowers. Disbud for larger blooms, deadhead spent heads, and feed with a high-potash fertilizer through summer.

planting.note: Set bulbs in autumn while soil is still workable so roots establish before hard freeze.

directions: Plant bulbs about 6 inches deep with the pointed end up in fertile, well-drained ground. Water in at planting, then leave dry until spring growth appears. Expect violet-purple goblet blooms in mid spring; let foliage yellow fully before removing it.

planting.note: Autumn planting gives the chilling period this Triumph needs to bloom well.

directions: Dig each bulb in about three times its height deep in a sunny bed. The blood-red petals edged in golden yellow open in mid spring on sturdy stems. Deadhead spent flowers but keep leaves intact to recharge the bulb.

planting.note: Get bulbs into cool autumn soil several weeks before it freezes solid.

directions: Bury bulbs 6 inches deep in loose, quick-draining soil and firm gently. Creamy-white petals feathered with purple margins appear in mid to late spring, the edging deepening as flowers age. Allow foliage to die back naturally after bloom.

planting.note: Plant in fall so the bulb roots before winter dormancy.

directions: Set bulbs about 6 inches deep in a well-drained, sunny spot. This old favorite opens rich crimson cups flushed with plum-purple on the outside in mid spring. Water sparingly during dormancy and lift only if summer soil stays wet.

planting.note: Autumn planting into cool soil is essential for spring flowering.

directions: Plant bulbs pointed side up, roughly 6 inches deep, in fertile ground with good drainage. Soft lilac-purple blooms with a paler base open in mid spring and hold well in the garden and vase. Leave the leaves until they brown so the bulb can rebuild.

planting.note: Set bulbs in fall a month or more before the ground freezes.

directions: Dig holes about 6 inches deep and space bulbs a hand-width apart. This early Triumph produces warm purple-violet cups in early to mid spring and naturalizes reliably. Keep beds on the dry side through summer dormancy.

planting.note: Plant in autumn while soil can still be dug easily.

directions: Bury bulbs about 6 inches deep in a bright, freely draining bed. Deep beetroot-red blooms with an almost black sheen open in mid spring. Snap off faded heads and let leaves ripen fully before tidying.

planting.note: Autumn is the window for planting these spring-flowering bulbs.

directions: Plant bulbs 6 inches deep and about 5 inches apart in sun and sharp drainage. Deep garnet-red cups with a satiny finish open in mid to late spring on strong stems. Water only during active growth and keep dormant bulbs dry.

planting.note: Plant in fall for early spring color.

directions: Set bulbs about 6 inches deep in loose soil with the tip facing up. Pointed, canary-yellow buds open to bright golden cups early in the season. Leave the foliage in place until it withers so the bulb stores energy for next year.

planting.note: Get bulbs into cool autumn ground several weeks ahead of freeze-up.

directions: Plant bulbs 6 inches deep in a sunny, well-drained bed. Vivid scarlet-red cups with a black-and-yellow base open in mid to late spring on tall, straight stems. Deadhead after bloom and let leaves yellow before cutting back.

planting.note: Plant in autumn so the large bulb roots before winter.

directions: Set the bulb about 7 inches deep in deep, fertile soil that drains freely. Big golden-yellow cups on stout stems open in mid spring and stand up well to weather. This vigorous Darwin Hybrid often perennializes if summers stay dry.

planting.note: Fall planting lets the bulb establish roots before the ground freezes.

directions: Plant bulbs 7 inches deep and about 6 inches apart in full sun. Large scarlet-red flowers with a faint yellow flush open in mid spring on tall, weatherproof stems. One of the more reliable tulips to return, especially in freely draining soil.

planting.note: Plant in fall for tall spring color.

directions: Bury the bulb roughly 7 inches deep in rich, well-drained soil. Golden-yellow petals boldly flamed and edged in red open in mid spring on strong stems. A striking, long-lasting Darwin Hybrid that stands up to wind and rain.

planting.note: Set bulbs in autumn ahead of hard frost.

directions: Plant bulbs about 7 inches deep in deep, fertile ground with sharp drainage. Very large rose-pink cups washed with soft salmon and cream open in mid spring on tall stems. Excellent for cutting; let the foliage ripen fully before removing.

planting.note: Plant in fall for some of the earliest tulip blooms.

directions: Set bulbs about 5 inches deep in a sheltered, sunny bed. Pure white, rounded single flowers open early in the season on short, sturdy stems. Their low height makes them good for the front of borders and containers.

planting.note: An heirloom best planted in cool autumn soil.

directions: Plant bulbs 5 to 6 inches deep in sun with free drainage. This centuries-old variety opens bright scarlet petals broadly edged in golden yellow in early spring. Deadhead after flowering and leave leaves to yellow naturally.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for early, peony-like spring flowers.

directions: Set bulbs about 5 inches deep in rich, well-drained soil. Full double blooms in warm rose-pink, deepening with age, open early on short stems. The heavy flowers appreciate a spot sheltered from strong wind.

planting.note: Plant in fall while the soil is still easy to work.

directions: Plant bulbs roughly 5 inches deep in a sunny, sheltered bed. Fully double, pure white flowers resembling small peonies open early in the season. Keep the top-heavy blooms out of strong wind and let foliage ripen after flowering.

planting.note: Autumn planting suits this early double.

directions: Set bulbs about 5 inches deep in fertile, freely draining soil. Rounded double blooms blend rose-pink and cream and open early on compact stems. Good in pots and border edges; protect the full flowers from battering rain.

planting.note: Plant in fall for late spring, lily-shaped flowers.

directions: Plant bulbs about 6 inches deep in sun and sharp drainage. Slender pointed petals in scarlet edged with clear yellow reflex outward in late spring. Site out of strong wind to protect the tall, graceful stems.

planting.note: Set bulbs in autumn for late spring bloom.

directions: Bury bulbs 6 inches deep in a bright bed with good drainage. White petals brushed with raspberry-red flames curve outward in late spring, each bloom uniquely marked. The tall stems make elegant cut flowers.

planting.note: Plant in fall for glossy late-spring color.

directions: Plant bulbs about 6 inches deep in full sun. Deep, glossy ruby-red petals taper to points and flare open in late spring on tall stems. Deadhead spent blooms and let leaves die back to feed next year's bulb.

planting.note: Autumn planting gives roots time to set before winter.

directions: Set bulbs 6 inches deep in sun and free-draining soil. Reddish-purple petals crisply edged in white reflex to form a star in late spring. The strong, tall stems are excellent for cutting.

planting.note: Plant in fall for deep late-spring color.

directions: Plant bulbs about 6 inches deep in a sunny, sharply drained bed. Rich purple-wine petals taper to points and open into a slim star in late spring. Group several bulbs together for the strongest effect.

planting.note: Set bulbs in autumn for tall late-spring flowers.

directions: Bury bulbs 6 inches deep in full sun with excellent drainage. Creamy-yellow petals feathered rose-pink at the edges reflex gracefully in late spring. The tall, slim stems make it a favorite for arrangements.
